spo_scope: In The Writing Revolution (Hochman & Wexler, 2017; 2nd ed. 2024), the Single-Paragraph Outline (SPO) is explicitly a planning tool for ONE paragraph. It is introduced in the chapter on single paragraphs (1st ed. Chapter 4, "A World of Possibility: The Single-Paragraph Outline"). Its structure is: one Topic Sentence line (written as a complete sentence), 3–4 numbered detail lines written in NOTE FORM (abbreviations, symbols, key words — not full sentences), and one Concluding Sentence line (also a complete sentence). TWR insists the T.S. and C.S. be full sentences while the dots/details stay in note form, precisely because the SPO's job is to organize the internal logic of a single paragraph before drafting it. Why it's the wrong tool for a whole essay: an essay is not one big paragraph. If you put a thesis on the SPO's topic-sentence line and three "body points" on its detail lines, you have (a) no plan for the introduction, (b) no topic sentence for any body paragraph, (c) no organized evidence WITHIN each body paragraph — the detail lines can only name a point, not structure its support — and (d) a "concluding sentence" line standing in for an entire conclusion paragraph. TWR's own progression treats the SPO as a prerequisite skill that must be mastered and then SCALED UP to a different, essay-level template — not stretched to cover the essay itself.
twr_essay_planner: TWR's essay-level planner is the Multiple-Paragraph Outline (MPO). It appears in the chapter on moving from single paragraphs to compositions (1st ed. Chapter 6, "Moving On to Compositions: Multiple-Paragraph Outlines"). Structure of the MPO for a standard essay: (1) At the top, the student writes the THESIS STATEMENT (in TWR's method, often developed first as a full sentence; for many prompts TWR has students draft the thesis before or alongside the outline, and the general statement/specific thesis pair is part of introduction work). (2) The outline then has a row/box for EACH paragraph of the essay: an Introduction box, one box per BODY paragraph, and a Conclusion box. (3) For each BODY paragraph, the student writes the paragraph's MAIN IDEA / topic in brief note form in a left "Main Idea" column, and then the SUPPORTING DETAILS for that paragraph in note form in the adjacent "Details" column — so each body paragraph gets its own mini-plan (main idea + its own evidence), which the student later converts into a topic sentence + developed support when drafting. (4) The Introduction and Conclusion boxes are planned using TWR's introduction/conclusion strategies (e.g., TWR's G.S.T. pattern for introductions: General statement → Specific statement → Thesis; and conclusion strategies that restate the thesis and broaden out). (5) TWR also emphasizes TRANSITIONS between paragraphs; the second edition and TWR training materials include a "Transition Outline" / transition-planning step, where students note the transition word/phrase or transition sentence linking each body paragraph to the next. The key contrast with the SPO: on the MPO, each detail line of what would have been a single paragraph becomes the MAIN IDEA of its own body paragraph, and each body paragraph then acquires its own set of supporting details.
spo_to_mpo_progression: TWR's sequence is deliberate and cumulative: (1) sentence-level activities (because/but/so, appositives, subordinating conjunctions, sentence expansion) build the syntactic moves needed for topic sentences, transitions, and elaboration; (2) students master the SPO — planning and drafting strong single paragraphs with a topic sentence, note-form details in logical order, and a concluding sentence; (3) the SPO is then used as the conceptual BUILDING BLOCK for the MPO. TWR makes the scaling-up explicit: a well-made SPO on a broad topic can be "exploded" into an MPO — each numbered detail on the SPO becomes the main idea of a body paragraph on the MPO, and the SPO's topic sentence grows into the essay's thesis. Hochman's stated logic is that students cannot plan multi-paragraph compositions until they can reliably structure one paragraph; the MPO is introduced only after SPO fluency, and even then TWR often starts with an "opinion/expository essay from a strong SPO" conversion exercise. The shift to essay-level planning happens at the MPO chapter (Ch. 6, 1st ed.), where introductions (G.S.T.), conclusions, and transitions between paragraphs are taught as NEW skills that the SPO never addressed — evidence in itself that TWR does not consider the SPO an essay planner.
source_anchors: Hochman & Wexler, The Writing Revolution (Jossey-Bass, 1st ed. 2017), Ch. 4 'A World of Possibility: The Single-Paragraph Outline' — SPO defined as single-paragraph planner; T.S./C.S. as full sentences, details in note form (approx. pp. 79–104 in 1st ed.)
Hochman & Wexler, The Writing Revolution, 1st ed., Ch. 5 'Putting Flesh on the Bones: Revising a draft' and paragraph revision work building on the SPO

Hochman & Wexler, The Writing Revolution, 1st ed., Ch. 6 'Moving On to Compositions: Multiple-Paragraph Outlines' — MPO template: thesis at top, main idea + details columns per body paragraph, introduction and conclusion planning (approx. pp. 135–170 in 1st ed.)
Same chapter (Ch. 6): G.S.T. (General–Specific–Thesis) introduction strategy and conclusion strategies; transitions between paragraphs
The Writing Revolution 2nd edition (2024) retains SPO→MPO sequence and adds expanded transition/Transition Outline material (chapter numbering differs from 1st ed.)
TWR training materials / thewritingrevolution.org resources: SPO and MPO templates distributed as distinct instruments; MPO template shows per-paragraph Main Idea + Details boxes
confidence: HIGH confidence on the core claims: the SPO is TWR's single-paragraph planner (topic sentence + note-form details + concluding sentence) and the Multiple-Paragraph Outline (MPO) is TWR's named essay-level planner with per-paragraph main-idea + details planning, plus separately taught introduction (G.S.T.) and conclusion strategies and transitions. These are central, unambiguous features of the book and TWR training. MEDIUM confidence on exact chapter titles and page ranges — I believe the SPO is Ch. 4 and the MPO chapter is Ch. 6 in the 1st edition ('Moving On to Compositions'), but the exact chapter numbers/titles and page numbers should be verified against the physical text, and 2nd-edition numbering differs. MEDIUM-LOW confidence on the precise term 'Transition Outline' as a formal named TWR instrument vs. transition planning being folded into the MPO chapter — flag for primary-text check. Also verify against the text whether TWR's MPO template has the thesis written at the top of the outline in all versions, and the exact TWR convention for whether body-paragraph main ideas on the MPO are note-form phrases (I believe yes) that are later converted into full topic sentences at drafting.
timeback_lesson_design: ONE IDEA: "An essay is planned paragraph-by-paragraph: a thesis at the top, then a plan for EVERY paragraph — intro, each body paragraph with its own main idea AND its own evidence, and a conclusion. A one-paragraph outline can't do that job." Slot sequence for the zero-JS gated-reading player (one segment at a time): (1) teach_card — state the one idea; name the MPO and contrast in one line: SPO plans one paragraph; MPO plans the whole essay, one row per paragraph. (2) stimulus_display — show a G9 prompt (e.g., 'Should schools require community service?') plus a COMPLETE worked MPO: thesis sentence at top; Intro box planned as G.S.T. notes (general statement → specific statement → thesis); Body 1/2/3 boxes each with a main-idea note AND 2–3 note-form evidence details of its own; Conclusion box with restated-thesis + broaden-out notes; transition words penciled between body boxes. (3) annotated_before_after — BEFORE: the flawed 'essay on an SPO' (thesis on the T.S. line, three bare points as dots, one concluding sentence). AFTER: the same content exploded into the MPO, with callouts showing each SPO dot becoming a body paragraph's main idea that now carries its own evidence, and intro/conclusion appearing for the first time. (4) discrimination — 3–4 pairs of outlines; student picks which is a real essay plan vs. a single-paragraph plan dressed up (tells: does each body point have its OWN evidence? is there an intro plan and conclusion plan, or just one sentence each?). (5) predict_the_fix — show a broken MPO (e.g., Body 2 has a main idea but no details, or evidence for Body 3 sitting under Body 1, or no intro plan); student predicts the specific fix before the reveal. (6) production_frq SUPPORTED — given a prompt, a ready thesis, and three main ideas, student fills the rest of the MPO: 2 note-form details per body paragraph + G.S.T. intro notes + conclusion notes. (7) production_frq INDEPENDENT — new prompt; student builds the full MPO from scratch: thesis, intro plan, 3 body boxes each with main idea + evidence details, conclusion plan, transitions. (8) production_frq TRANSFER (optional) — student is handed a strong SPO on a broad topic and must explode it into an MPO (the exact TWR conversion move). (9) diagnosis_frq — student explains in 1–2 sentences why the original SPO-as-essay-plan fails (no per-paragraph evidence, no intro/conclusion plan). (10) self_score — checklist: full-sentence thesis at top? one box per paragraph? each body box has main idea + its own note-form details? intro planned G.S.T.? conclusion restates thesis and broadens? transitions noted? Details in NOTE form, not full sentences (TWR convention).
what_the_current_lesson_gets_wrong: Precise errors: (1) Tool misassignment — it uses the SPO, TWR's single-paragraph planner, to plan a multi-paragraph essay; TWR reserves essay planning for the Multiple-Paragraph Outline. (2) Category error on the detail lines — the lesson's 'three ordered body POINTS, each naming its evidence' compresses an entire body paragraph (which needs a topic sentence PLUS multiple organized supporting details) into one note line; a body paragraph cannot be planned by a single line that 'names' its evidence. (3) Missing paragraphs — the SPO frame gives no plan for the introduction (TWR teaches G.S.T.: general → specific → thesis) or the conclusion as a full paragraph, and no transitions between body paragraphs. (4) Progression violation — TWR treats the SPO as the mastered building block that gets EXPANDED into the MPO (each SPO dot becomes a body paragraph's main idea); the lesson instead freezes the essay at the SPO stage. THE FIX: replace the SPO frame with the MPO. The corrected plan is: thesis sentence at the top; an intro box planned via G.S.T.; three body boxes, each with a note-form MAIN IDEA (later converted to a topic sentence) and its OWN 2–3 note-form evidence details; a conclusion box (restate thesis, broaden); transitions noted between bodies. Keep the TWR convention that outline details are note form while thesis (and later topic sentences) are full sentences. Pedagogically, the strongest teaching move is to show the explosion: the flawed SPO-as-essay-plan side-by-side with the MPO it should become.
